mnemonics.title: RBAC Components
mnemonics.content: Remember RBAC as 'URP' - Users have Roles assigned with Permissions. Users → Roles → Permissions flow is the core relationship.

mnemonics.title: Least Privilege
mnemonics.content: Least Privilege = 'MINIMUM ACCESS' - Only what you NEED, not what you WANT. Think of it as 'Need to Know' basis.

mnemonics.title: Separation of Duties
mnemonics.content: SOD = 'Share the Load' - Don't let one person control everything. Split critical tasks between multiple roles to prevent fraud.

mnemonics.title: Role Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Think of role hierarchy like an organizational chart - managers inherit all permissions from their subordinates, simplifying administration.

acronyms.usage: Access control model combining DAC flexibility with RBAC structure
Access control model based on user roles within an organization
Access control where owners discretionarily grant access to others
Access control enforced by the system based on classification levels
Process of assigning users to appropriate roles
Process of assigning permissions to roles
Security principle preventing single-point control over critical tasks

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with Users at bottom, Roles in middle, Permissions at top. Arrows flow upward from Users→Roles→Permissions showing how access is granted through role abstraction.
Think of an organizational chart tree. CEO role at top inherits all permissions from Manager, which inherits from Employee. It's like inheriting authority downward.
Imagine overlapping circles representing roles. When a user has multiple roles, all circles combine (union) to show total permissions.
Picture a series of locked doors, each representing a permission. Users only get keys to doors they absolutely need for their job - not master keys.

analogies.analogy: Roles in RBAC are like job titles - a 'Manager' role has certain responsibilities (permissions) just as a manager in a company has specific duties. You don't give everyone manager status.
Think of permissions as physical keys. Each key (permission) opens a specific door (resource). Giving someone a key to only the rooms they need is least privilege.

analogies.concept: Role Hierarchy as Chain of Command
analogies.analogy: Role hierarchy is like a military chain of command - a general has all the authority of colonels, who have all the authority of captains, etc.
DRBAC is like a club where members (users) join specific groups (roles) and get club privileges (permissions), but resource owners can also invite guests (discretionary element).
